metadata.summary: Import convention for Django multi-app projects: always alias cross-app imports, never direct symbol import. Eliminates model name collisions and enables reliable tooling.

content.title: Django Import Patterns
content.summary: A convention for Django multi-app projects: always import cross-app modules via alias (from apps.orders import models as order_models), never via direct symbol import (from apps.orders.models import Order). Import order: __future__, stdlib, third-party (Django/DRF), cross-app aliased, relative. Type-only cross-app imports go under if TYPE_CHECKING: with from __future__ import annotations at the file top.

why.p: In Django projects with many apps, model names collide: User, Order, Item appear in multiple apps. Direct imports create ambiguity in call sites and make grep-based refactoring unreliable. The alias convention (order_models.Order) makes the source app explicit at every usage, eliminates name collisions, and enables reliable tooling (import-linter, AST fixers) to enforce cross-app coupling constraints.

list.item: New Django project: lock down import style before apps proliferate.
Onboarding multi-app codebases where Order, User, Item collide in serializers.
Refactoring legacy code with from app.models import * patterns.
Adding type hints to a Django codebase with circular import problems.
Authoring pre-commit hooks to enforce the alias rule.
Single-app Django projects with fewer than 10 models — alias overhead pays nothing.
Non-Django Python repos with flat package structure — rule is Django-app-specific.
DRF serializers and form classes where direct symbol import is idiomatic (from rest_framework import serializers) — the rule applies to cross-app project imports only.
One-shot scripts and Jupyter notebooks where unaliased names are more readable.

section.name: limitations-and-failure-modes
section.p: No automated enforcement out of the box. ruff/isort sort imports but do not enforce the alias-on-cross-app rule; teams drift unless a custom AST checker runs in CI.
Alias bikeshedding: order_models vs orders_models vs m_orders — without a written convention every PR re-litigates the alias.
Long lines: order_models.OrderLineItemDiscount reads worse than OrderLineItemDiscount; the rule trades readability for unambiguity.

TYPE_CHECKING + circular imports. The convention mentions from __future__ import annotations but does not show how it interacts with serializer Meta.model (which needs runtime symbols). Easy to break.
DRF / Django generics use direct imports by convention. Mixing the two styles in one file (alias for app models, direct for DRF) confuses readers and tools.
Refactor cost. Renaming an app (apps.orders → apps.commerce) cascades into every alias usage; a git grep works but agents still miss usages in tests and migrations.

rule.statement: Import cross-app modules via their module alias: from apps.orders import models as order_models. Never import symbols directly: from apps.orders.models import Order.
rule.rationale: Django projects with many apps accumulate model name collisions: User, Order, Item appear in multiple apps. Aliases make the source app explicit at every call site and enable reliable automated refactoring.

rule.statement: Use a consistent alias suffix per module type: _models, _services, _constants. Never mix order_models and orders_models in the same project.
rule.rationale: Inconsistent alias names cause the same name-collision problem the convention exists to solve. Pin the pattern in pyproject.toml documentation and enforce via AST linter.

rule.statement: Order imports in four tiers: (1) __future__, (2) stdlib, (3) third-party (django, rest_framework, celery), (4) cross-app aliased, (5) relative from . import.
rule.rationale: PEP 8 / isort canonical order. Consistent ordering eliminates diff noise and makes it trivial to see which tier an import belongs to at a glance.

rule.statement: Never use wildcard imports: from apps.orders.models import * is unconditionally banned.
rule.rationale: Wildcards make it impossible to determine which names are in scope, break tools like mypy and pylint, and re-create the name-collision problem the alias rule solves.

rule.statement: Gate type-only cross-app imports behind if TYPE_CHECKING: and add from __future__ import annotations at the file top.
rule.rationale: Cross-app imports between models.py files are the primary source of Django circular imports. TYPE_CHECKING is False at runtime, so the import never executes. __future__ annotations defers annotation evaluation, making the type hint work without the runtime import.

rule.statement: DRF serializer Meta.model requires a runtime symbol; do not gate it under TYPE_CHECKING. Use apps.get_model("app_label", "ModelName") or a direct runtime import.
rule.rationale: Serializers introspect Meta.model at class creation time. A TYPE_CHECKING-gated import makes the symbol undefined at runtime, causing an AttributeError on serializer load.

rule.statement: Exclude migrations/ from the alias linter. Django's migration loader parses model references as strings; agent-generated alias rewrites in migrations break the loader.
rule.rationale: Migration files use "users.User" string references that are resolved by Django's app registry, not Python imports. Rewriting them to use_models.User breaks makemigrations and migrate.

example.title: Canonical Import Block
example.code:
from __future__ import annotations from typing import TYPE_CHECKING from datetime import datetime, timedelta from decimal import Decimal
from django.db import models from rest_framework import serializers
from apps.orders import models as order_models from apps.users import models as user_models from apps.catalog import models as catalog_models from apps.orders import services as order_services from apps.orders import constants as order_constants
from .models import MyModel from . import constants
if TYPE_CHECKING: from apps.payments import models as payment_models
example.note: Five tiers in order. Cross-app aliased in tier 4. TYPE_CHECKING-only imports in a separate block at the bottom.

example.title: Direct Import (Avoid)
example.code: from apps.users.models import User # avoid: direct symbol import from apps.orders.models import Order # avoid: direct symbol import from apps.users.models import * # NEVER: wildcard
example.note: Direct imports cause name collisions and break alias-based tooling. Wildcard imports are unconditionally banned.

example.title: TYPE_CHECKING for Circular Dependency
example.code:
from __future__ import annotations from typing import TYPE_CHECKING
if TYPE_CHECKING: from apps.billing import models as billing_models
def get_invoice_total(user: billing_models.Invoice) -> Decimal: """Works in Python 3.9+ with __future__ annotations.""" ...
example.note: __future__ annotations defers evaluation so billing_models.Invoice is never resolved at runtime. No circular import.

example.title: Python 3.10+ Modern Syntax
example.note: Use built-in generics (list[], dict[], tuple[]) and | for union types in Python 3.10+. Still use cross-app aliases.

antipattern.description: Direct cross-app model import: from apps.users.models import User.
antipattern.reason: In projects with multiple apps each defining a User model, direct imports create silent name collisions. The alias convention makes the source app explicit at every usage and enables reliable tooling.
Wildcards make namespace scope opaque to tools (mypy, pylint, IDEs), prevent refactoring automation, and collapse the alias convention's disambiguation benefit.
Re-exports collapse the app namespace and recreate the original collision problem. Consumers can import from the wrong location silently. Keep __init__.py empty unless explicitly justified.

antipattern.description: DRF serializer Meta.model gated under TYPE_CHECKING.
antipattern.reason: Serializer Meta.model is resolved at class creation time (import time). A TYPE_CHECKING-gated import makes the symbol undefined at runtime, causing an AttributeError on serializer load. Use apps.get_model() or a runtime import for Meta.model.

antipattern.description: Agent rewrites import aliases but misses call sites: User.objects.get() not updated to user_models.User.objects.get().
antipattern.reason: Partial rewrites leave dangling names that cause NameError at runtime. Alias fixes require AST-based rewrite of both the import and every usage, not just the import line.

antipattern.description: Inconsistent alias names across files: user_models in one file, users_models in another.
antipattern.reason: Inconsistency defeats the readability benefit of the convention. Every new contributor must re-discover the alias for each app. Pin one suffix pattern (_models, _services, _constants) project-wide.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Use Claude subagents in three modes against this convention. (1) Scaffolder — when generating a new app or models module, the agent emits the import block in the canonical order (stdlib → third-party → cross-app aliased → relative) and pre-imports the app's own models, services, constants siblings. (2) Linter / fixer — an agent reads existing files, detects from apps.X.models import Y violations, and rewrites them to from apps.X import models as X_models plus call-site changes. (3) Reviewer — on PRs, the agent confirms no import *, no unaliased cross-app, no missing TYPE_CHECKING for circular dependencies.

Best practice: Require agents to read conftest or lint output first. Prompt the agent to use import-linter config + AST diff to enforce the alias rule and the order-by-tier rule. Never allow an import-fixing agent to rewrite without also rewriting all call sites — partial fixes produce NameError at runtime.

section.name: ai-agent-gotchas
section.p: Default LLM output is direct imports. Models trained on tutorials produce from apps.users.models import User. Always pin the prompt to the alias rule and run the linter post-generation.
Alias name drift. Agents pick users_models one file and user_models the next. Force a canonical pattern and reject diffs that deviate.
Removing an alias usage breaks call sites. Agents that fix imports often forget to update User → user_models.User references downstream. Require an AST-based rewrite, not a regex one.
Generated __init__.py re-exports. Agents creating an __init__.py like to populate __all__ from submodules — that defeats the alias intent. Keep __init__.py empty unless explicitly justified.
Circular imports masked by TYPE_CHECKING. Agents wrap runtime imports in the gate when they should not; runtime breakage at module load. Verify by booting Django (manage.py check) after agent edits.
DRF serializer Meta.model requires runtime symbol. Agents put it under TYPE_CHECKING; serializer fails at import. Guard with a runtime import or apps.get_model().

Migrations rewriting. Migrations generated by makemigrations reference models by string; an over-eager import-fixer agent may rewrite them to use the alias and break Django's migration loader. Exclude migrations/ paths.
Wildcard re-imports. Agents trained on from .views import * patterns reintroduce wildcards into urls.py. Lint blocks this.
isort vs ruff conflict. Agents installing isort separately produce different sort orders than ruff; pick one tool (ruff) and disable the other in pre-commit.

apps ambiguity. Some Django projects keep apps at the top level (users/, not apps/users/). The rule must be re-templated; an agent reusing the README verbatim emits from apps.X against the wrong layout.

section.name: best-practices
section.p: Pin the alias convention in pyproject.toml under [tool.ruff.lint.isort] known-first-party = ["apps"] and document the alias suffix (_models, _services, _constants).
Never re-export models from __init__.py. It collapses the alias rule and re-creates the original collision problem.
Use TYPE_CHECKING aggressively for cross-app type hints. Combined with from __future__ import annotations, it eliminates 90% of circular import problems.
Migrations must use string app labels ("users.User", not from apps.users.models import User); enforce this with a separate ruff rule on */migrations/*.
Keep settings.py free of app imports. Lazy resolve with django.apps.apps.get_model("users", "User") when needed at runtime.
One alias per app per module — do not mix user_models and users_models in the same file.
Document forbidden direction. apps.users should not import from apps.orders; encode in import-linter contracts. Domain coupling enforced at import level prevents architectural rot.
Refactor in two steps: first add the new alias and run, then mechanically delete the old direct imports.

template.purpose: AST linter: flags cross-app imports without alias and wildcard imports.
